biases.name: Density axis
biases.rule: prefer 1-2 attacks per beat; reserve 3-4 attacks for arrivals (phrase ends, cadences, climaxes)

biases.name: Vertical spread axis
biases.rule: prefer 3-5 octave spread; avoid SATB-like 2-octave middle-register cluster
biases.penalty_target: narrow spread in middle register for sustained passages

biases.name: SATB-zone avoidance
biases.rule: penalize combinations with high density + narrow spread + middle register + on-the-beat block attacks

biases.name: Expose the extremes
biases.rule: the audience tracks the highest and the lowest voice — keep both clear. Prefer voicings where the top (melody) and the bottom (bass) are registrally isolated from the middle; put color in the middle instead of close-position chord tones directly under the melody
biases.penalty_target: (a) melody + plus_1/plus_2 cluster just below the top; (b) bass crowded into mid-register fill rather than sitting low; (c) no LH strike below string 15 (C3) for the entire hymn

dimensions.name: Shape selection under the melody note
dimensions.note: WHICH chord sits under the melody. The 118-fraction pool is a vocabulary of harp-specific chord shapes: 42 'path' fractions that walk the six trefoil cycles (2nds/3rds/4ths × CW/CCW), and 76 'reserve' fractions held for color and substitution. The melody note is always the top voice — tactics in this dimension pick whether the shape below it is a full 4-finger pool chord, a quartal stack, a triad, a sparse dyad, or nothing at all. Strictly diatonic: no tritone subs, no modal interchange, no chromatic alterations.

dimensions.name: Register placement
dimensions.note: Which octave(s) the chord lives in. A pedal harp has 47 strings spanning 7 octaves; the same shape played low vs. high has a very different color — low is boomy and fundamental-heavy, high is bright and partial-heavy, middle-register stacking produces the 'muddy' sound. Tactics here shift the shape up or down relative to the melody note, or spread it across two octaves. Related to but distinct from 'range' (which is about the whole piece).

dimensions.name: Non-melody hand activity
dimensions.note: What the LH actually does beat-by-beat. On a harp, strings ring after plucking, so the LH doesn't need to restrike to keep a chord sounding. 'Strike and ring' is the harp-idiomatic default (one LH attack on beat 1, no restrike); 'partial silence' is jazz-style sparse comping (e.g. hits on 2-and + 4); 'low_bass_grab' is a grace-note dive to sub-bass before the main attack; 'none' is melody-alone in the RH.

dimensions.name: Melody hand activity beyond the melody note
dimensions.note: What the RH adds around the melody. Doubling the melody an octave up, double-stops (two-note chord with the melody on top), inner-voice fills, broken-chord figuration under the melody, running 16ths, or nothing beyond the melody note itself. More RH activity = more perceived density; too much competes with the LH.

dimensions.name: Connection from previous measure
dimensions.note: How the current measure's opening relates to the previous measure's ending. Because the harp articulates every note (plucked, not bowed), perceived continuity across bar lines comes from voicing choice, not legato phrasing. Tactics include common-tone pivot (a pitch from the previous chord held into this one), approach-from-larger/smaller interval, release (let the previous ring out without competition), and chromatic neighbor approach.

dimensions.name: Connection to next measure
dimensions.note: Mirror of connect_from — how this measure's ending sets up the next. The same vocabulary applies (common tones, approach tones, release, larger/smaller intervals), but on the outgoing side of the bar line. A strong connect_to + a strong connect_from on the next bar = smooth phrase; both missing = each bar feels isolated.

dimensions.name: Harmonic substitution at measure scale
dimensions.note: Replacing the expected diatonic chord with a related one. In this strictly-diatonic system the legal subs are: quality swap (V ↔ V7, I ↔ IΔ), deceptive (V → vi, V → VI in minor), third-substitution (ii → IV, the chord a third above), and common-tone pivot (swap to any chord that shares ≥2 pitches). NOT legal: tritone sub, ♭II7, altered dominants, harmonic-minor V (handled separately by four fallback strategies). Every sub must keep the melody note playable in the new shape.

dimensions.name: Density
dimensions.note: How many notes are sounding at once. Sparse (1–2 notes), medium (3–4), dense (5+). Harp resonance rewards sparseness: because strings sustain, fewer simultaneous notes lets each be heard; stacking too many produces harmonic mud. Density interacts with register — a dense chord low is muddier than the same chord high.

dimensions.name: Texture
dimensions.note: The sonic character of the measure independent of which notes are played. Block chord (all notes attacked together), rolled/arpeggiated (staggered low-to-high, the harp-idiomatic default), staggered (rolled more slowly, a conscious ornament), bisbigliando (whispered tremolo on adjacent strings in unison tuning), single-line (melody alone, no harmony). Texture choice shapes perceived rhythm.

dimensions.name: Lever state
dimensions.note: Lever / pedal harp configuration — which strings are flipped to accidentals. On a lever harp, each lever raises one string a semitone; on a pedal harp, each pedal raises (or lowers) every string of that letter. Changes cost a physical gesture (and time). Tactics here: don't flip (stay in the starting key), flip-and-back (brief accidental then restore), flip-hold (a modulation-like shift that persists), use-flipped (exploit an already-engaged lever), prepare-next (change a lever now for a use one bar later).

dimensions.name: Range exploitation
dimensions.note: How widely the arrangement spans the harp's range across the whole piece. 'Stay' (narrow band, typically middle register), migrate_up / migrate_down (gradual drift over several bars), split (LH low + RH high with a deliberate gap between), extreme (uses the lowest sub-bass and highest top octaves). Distinct from 'register' — register is a per-measure octave choice; range is about the trajectory.

dimensions.name: Measure role in phrase
dimensions.note: The function of this measure within its phrase. Opening (phrase first bar, establishes the harmony), middle (sustains momentum, variety), cadence_approach (penultimate, builds tension — typically more density/dissonance), cadence (the resolution), release (post-cadence settle, typically sparse). Different tactics are appropriate at different phrase positions; e.g. a dense RH fill fits cadence_approach but clashes on a release bar.

tactics.name: Full 4-finger library shape
tactics.note: The default pool shape — all 4 notes of the library fraction under the melody. Most resonant on a harp because every string contributes a partial; best for full hymn statements. Becomes muddy in the low register or when the density is already high.

tactics.name: 3-finger (drop one chord tone)
tactics.note: Drop one chord tone (usually the 5th, since its overtone is already implied by the root). The remaining three notes breathe and the top voice is sharper — a cleaner version of full_4 that keeps the harmonic color but lightens the texture.

tactics.name: 2-finger shell: 3rd + 7th
tactics.note: Just the 3rd and 7th — the 'guide tones' that define the chord's quality. Rootless, transparent; idiomatic when the melody is busy or when a bass line (or a duet partner) provides the root elsewhere.

tactics.name: 2-finger shell: root + 10th
tactics.note: Root + 10th (3rd an octave above the root). The 10th is wide enough to ring without beating against the root. Strong, open sound — good as a bar opener when you want the root stated and one color tone stacked above.

tactics.name: No left hand (melody alone)
tactics.note: Melody alone in the RH, no left hand. Rare in hymnody — useful on a release bar after a cadence, or as a dramatic pullback mid-phrase. Conflicts with every lh_activity except `none`.

tactics.name: Quartal-tagged library shape
tactics.note: A quartal-tagged pool fraction (stacked 4ths rather than 3rds). Cooler and more modern-sounding than a triad; useful where an ambiguous color helps the melody float rather than sit on a clear tonic/dominant.

tactics.name: Sus-tagged library shape
tactics.note: A sus-tagged pool fraction — the 4 replaces the 3. Unresolved-sounding; wants to fall into a triadic chord on the next bar, so it's a natural fit for cadence_approach.

tactics.name: Shape with added 9
tactics.note: Adds the 9 (2nd up an octave) to the base fraction. Jazzier and a little sweeter. The 9 must not clash with the melody note — the selector will skip this when the melody is on the 3rd (half step away).

tactics.name: Shape with added 11
tactics.note: Adds the 11 (4 up an octave). Slightly sour against a major 3rd; usually better on minor chords where the 11 rings consonantly. Watch for the avoid-note interaction with the melody.

tactics.name: Shape with added 13
tactics.note: Adds the 13 (6 up an octave). Rich color, especially on dominant chords as a natural upper extension. On major triads it reads as a 'jazzy 6' chord.

tactics.name: Same register as previous measure
tactics.note: Keep the shape in the same register band as the previous bar. The ear tracks the harmony as one continuous voice rather than a jump — the unmarked continuity choice.

tactics.name: One octave lower
tactics.note: Drop the whole shape an octave. Darkens the color and adds body; useful to add weight on a cadence, a phrase arrival, or to reset after the piece has drifted high.

tactics.name: One octave higher
tactics.note: Raise the whole shape an octave. Brighter and lighter — the complement of down_oct. Natural after a release bar, or to open a new verse with more sparkle.

tactics.name: Drop bass to sub-bass, keep rest
tactics.note: Drop just the bass note to the sub-bass (C2 and below) while keeping the upper voices in place. Gives a huge low-frequency foundation without thinning the chord color.

tactics.name: Wide spread: bass low, melody high, empty middle
tactics.note: Bass low, melody high, nothing in the middle. Very open, concerto-like sound — rings long because the two registers don't interfere. Use it when you want the melody to float clearly above a distant bass.

tactics.name: Compressed: both hands within two octaves
tactics.note: Bass and melody within two octaves. Risky on SATB-style hymns — the close voicing can stack into muddy mid-range, but works well for a single intimate moment.

tactics.name: Sustain the shape (one attack, let ring)
tactics.note: One LH attack on beat 1 of the bar, no release until the next measure. Minimal effort, maximum ring — the harp-native default. Nearly identical to strike_and_ring but framed as a deliberate musical choice rather than inherited behavior.

tactics.name: Arpeggiate low to high
tactics.note: Roll the LH shape low-to-high across the bar. Gentle and lyrical; matches the harp stereotype. Pairs well with a sustained RH melody.

tactics.name: Arpeggiate high to low
tactics.note: Roll the LH shape high-to-low. Less common than arp_up, more dramatic — feels like a descent or a waterfall. Good on a release bar or a dynamic fade.

tactics.name: Bass-chord-chord rhythm
tactics.note: Bass on beat 1, chord on beats 2 and 3 (in 3/4) or bass-chord-chord-chord in 4/4. The stride-piano idiom borrowed onto the harp — gives a clear pulse without overwhelming the melody.

tactics.name: Chord-only on offbeats
tactics.note: Chord strikes only on offbeats (the 'ands'). Syncopated feel; works best when the melody or a partner voice carries the downbeat.

tactics.name: Tremolo between two shape tones
tactics.note: Rapid alternation between two tones of the shape. Atmospheric color — reserve for a single dramatic bar, not continuous use, or the effect wears out.

tactics.name: No LH activity (because no LH shape)
tactics.note: label tactic — selector picks this exactly when shape.no_lh is chosen, so the lh_activity dimension always has a legal pick

tactics.name: Partial silence — LH strikes on some beats, rests on others
tactics.note: classic sparse-comping pattern: LH hits e.g. beat 2-and + beat 4-and, rests on downbeats while the ring carries

tactics.name: Single strike at bar start, let harp ring
tactics.note: harp-idiomatic default: one LH attack on beat 1, no restrikes; the long-sustain of harp strings fills the measure

tactics.name: Countermelody stepwise within the shape
tactics.note: LH walks stepwise inside the shape, forming a secondary line against the melody. Adds polyphonic interest; expensive in attention and can crowd the melody if the ranges are too close.

tactics.name: Low-bass grace note before LH shape strum
tactics.note: grace-note dive to a very low bass (e.g. sub-bass octave) played just before the LH chord strum; an ornament that anchors the bottom of the texture

tactics.name: Melody alone, one finger
tactics.note: Just the melody line, one finger. The most transparent RH option — pairs naturally with a busy LH that carries the harmonic content.

tactics.name: Melody + one chord tone below
tactics.note: Melody with one chord tone below it. The classic 'melody and one harmony note' texture — gives the top line weight without sacrificing clarity or colliding with the LH.

tactics.name: Melody + two chord tones below
tactics.note: Melody with two chord tones below (a triad topped by the melody). Fuller but denser; the bottom note must clear the LH top (gap rule) or the voices will collide on the same string group.

tactics.name: Melody doubled at the octave
tactics.note: Melody doubled at the octave. Bright and strong; ideal for a climactic phrase or a final refrain. Costs the RH a wider reach — harder on fast passages.

tactics.name: Melody with diatonic neighbor ornament
tactics.note: Ornament the melody with a diatonic upper or lower neighbor note (e.g. hymn-tune C turns into C-D-C or C-B-C). Adds motion without changing the tune.

tactics.name: Melody anticipating next measure's chord tone
tactics.note: Melody leans into the next measure's chord — plays a chord tone of the coming chord before the bar line. Creates forward pull, useful at phrase joints.

tactics.name: Melody held, inner finger adds passing tone
tactics.note: Inner RH finger walks a diatonic passing tone between melody notes. Smooths wide melodic leaps without altering the melody itself.

tactics.name: Same shape repeated (zero motion)
tactics.note: Repeat the previous bar's shape — zero motion. Stasis; good on a held syllable where the text doesn't want motion, or to emphasize a single harmony across two bars.

tactics.name: Same shape inverted
tactics.note: Same pitches, different inversion — the bass note reshuffled. Minimal motion with a small bass-line interest that avoids total stasis.

tactics.name: Shape shifted by step
tactics.note: Shape shifted by a step (2nd up or down). The smoothest voice-leading option — every voice moves by step or stays.

tactics.name: Shape shifted by third
tactics.note: Shape shifted by a third. Common-tone-rich (usually 2 pitches carry over). Warm, jazz-ballad feel — used heavily by the substitution.diatonic pathway.

tactics.name: Shape shifted by larger interval
tactics.note: Shape shifted by a 4th, 5th, or wider. More dramatic motion — works for emphasis on a cadence_approach bar or a phrase-boundary arrival.

tactics.name: Shape released, new shape struck fresh
tactics.note: Previous shape cut off cleanly before the new one strikes. Articulates the bar line; useful after a rest in the melody or a breath mark in the text.

tactics.name: Shape sustained, new activity added
tactics.note: Previous shape still ringing when the new bar starts; new activity (LH figuration, extension) layers on top. Continuity with added motion — requires the new shape to share pitches with the old one.

tactics.name: Land on target shape by downbeat
tactics.note: Default — the target shape hits on beat 1 of the next bar, on time. The reference point that the other connect_to tactics deliberately depart from.

tactics.name: Anticipate target shape on last beat
tactics.note: Target shape comes in early — usually on the last 8th of this bar, before the new downbeat. Pushes forward, creates a syncopated feel into the new bar.

tactics.name: Delay arrival to beat 2 of next measure
tactics.note: Target shape arrives on beat 2 of the next bar instead of beat 1. The old harmony hangs briefly over the new downbeat, creating a suspension feel that resolves on beat 2.

tactics.name: Approach target by stepwise bass
tactics.note: A bass-line stepwise approach into the target (scalar bass motion ending on the target root). The walking-bass idiom — common in hymns under a descending or ascending bass line.

tactics.name: Approach target by common-tone pivot
tactics.note: Pivot through a shared pitch to the target. The ear tracks the common tone as a held voice while the rest moves — good for a smooth modulation inside a phrase.

tactics.name: Use the written chord's library shape
tactics.note: No substitution — use the written chord's pool shape as-is. The correct default for hymnody; honors the composer's harmonization.

tactics.name: Diatonic substitute (vi for I, iii for I, IV for ii)
tactics.note: Swap for a diatonic substitute that shares two or more pitches: vi for I (same except root→6), iii for I (same except top→3), IV for ii. Preserves harmonic function without adding any chromatic tension.

tactics.name: Inversion for stepwise bass
tactics.note: Invert the chord (first or second inversion) to put a non-root note in the bass. Creates a stepwise bass line through the phrase while leaving the melody and upper voices untouched.

tactics.name: Pedal point: hold previous chord, let melody imply new harmony
tactics.note: Hold the previous chord's bass; the melody implies the new harmony but the bass doesn't move. Tension builds until the pedal resolves — classic at cadence_approach into a final cadence.

tactics.name: Sus version of the written chord
tactics.note: Replace the 3 of the chord with the 4 (sus4). Neither major nor minor — always wants to resolve, so pair with a clean landing on the next chord (usually the same root, re-triaded).

tactics.name: Delay the chord change
tactics.note: Postpone the chord change by a beat or two into the bar. The ear hears the old chord hang briefly before the new one arrives — a written-out version of a suspension.

tactics.name: One attack total (sustain)
tactics.note: A single attack covering the whole bar — sustain throughout. Sparsest option; relies on the harp's long decay to carry the measure.

tactics.name: One attack per beat
tactics.note: One attack per beat (4 per 4/4 bar, 3 per 3/4). Steady, hymn-appropriate, easy to sing along with. The unmarked default for chorale texture.

tactics.name: Two attacks per beat
tactics.note: Eighth-note subdivision — two attacks per beat. Denser; starts to risk SATB-overlap mud on a chorale because notes are refreshed before the previous ring has decayed.

tactics.name: Syncopated: offbeats only
tactics.note: Attacks only on offbeats (the 'ands'). Creates a groove feel that's rare in sacred music — use sparingly, as it changes the character away from hymn.

tactics.name: Front-loaded
tactics.note: Activity concentrated on the first half of the bar, decays to silence in the second half. Good opening attack, weak ending — pair with a strong connect_to to bridge the gap.

tactics.name: Back-loaded
tactics.note: Reverse of front_loaded — quiet first half, action at the end. Sets up the next bar by building tension into the bar line.

tactics.name: Block attacks (hands together)
tactics.note: Hands strike together — chord on the beat, nothing in between. Sharp, declamatory; the pure chorale texture. Watch for SATB over-alignment if all four voices land on every beat.

tactics.name: Staggered attacks (hands alternate)
tactics.note: Hands alternate attacks (LH then RH, or split on different beats). Creates an implicit pulse without literal syncopation — the ear infers rhythm from the alternation.

tactics.name: Arpeggiated across both hands
tactics.note: Full arpeggio across both hands, low to high. The quintessential 'harp sound' — every note speaks in sequence rather than simultaneously. Longest-duration texture.

tactics.name: Rolled chord
tactics.note: A roll (arpeggiated chord, marked with the wavy vertical line in notation). Shorter than arp_both — just unstacks the attack by ~20ms per note so the chord has a harp-idiomatic sweep rather than a piano-like slam.

tactics.name: Bisbigliando on two tones
tactics.note: Whisper-fast tremolo between two strings tuned to the same pitch (requires pedal-harp enharmonic equivalents, e.g. Cb/B). Purely coloristic — reserve for a single held melody note where you want a shimmering background.

tactics.name: Single-line only
tactics.note: One melodic line, no harmony beneath. melody_alone and melody_oct are both compatible (octave-doubling still reads as a single line). Close-position doubling (melody_plus_1, melody_plus_2) is not — it becomes the 'kid banging' cluster the teacher warned about.

tactics.name: No flip
tactics.note: Stay in the starting key's lever setup. No physical gestures required beyond playing — the default for diatonic hymnody.

tactics.name: One flip at start, flip back at end
tactics.note: Flip one lever at the bar start, play the accidental, flip it back before the end. Brief chromatic excursion — expensive in hand motion (two gestures) but lets you touch a non-diatonic tone.

tactics.name: One flip at start, stay flipped
tactics.note: Flip a lever and leave it engaged for a section. Effectively a mini-modulation; saves gestures later at the cost of a flip-back at the section boundary.

tactics.name: Use already-flipped state
tactics.note: Exploit a lever already engaged from a previous bar (a note already sharpened or flatted). A 'free lunch' — no new gesture required, you just play the pre-flipped string.

tactics.name: Prepare next measure's flip
tactics.note: Flip a lever in this bar for a note that won't sound until the next bar. Pre-positioning — legal because harp levers can be engaged silently while other strings are playing.

tactics.name: Stay in current register band
tactics.note: Hold the current register band — both hands stay in their current octave zones across the phrase. Calm, grounded; the arrangement doesn't wander.

tactics.name: Migrate up
tactics.note: Both hands drift upward over several bars. Builds energy; useful into a climactic phrase or a key change that leans higher.

tactics.name: Migrate down
tactics.note: Both hands drift downward across several bars. Settles the piece; good for a final verse or an ending that wants to come to rest low.

tactics.name: Split: one hand stays, one moves
tactics.note: One hand stays in register, the other migrates. Opens the texture — the stationary hand acts as an anchor while the other explores a distant register.

tactics.name: Reach to extreme for color
tactics.note: rare

tactics.name: Opening of phrase
tactics.note: The phrase-starting bar. Establishes the key chord cleanly — typically sparser than the cadence, with a clear unambiguous harmonic statement so the ear knows where it is.

tactics.name: Middle of phrase
tactics.note: Interior bars of the phrase. Sustain momentum and offer variety. Most freely ornamented position — the place where color tactics (add9/11/13, substitutions) can land without disturbing the phrase arc.

tactics.name: Cadence approach (V→I or V→vi)
tactics.note: Penultimate bar of the phrase — traditionally V or V7 heading into the cadence. Maximum harmonic tension: good place for an extension, a sus chord, or a substitution that heightens the pull into the final bar.

tactics.name: Cadence itself
tactics.note: The resolution bar of the phrase (typically I, or the deceptive vi/VI). Consonant, grounded; sparser than the approach to let the resolution breathe.

tactics.name: Post-cadence release
tactics.note: The first bar after the cadence, before the next phrase starts. Often shorter than expected, so the arrangement pulls back — fewer notes, quieter dynamic, sometimes just the melody alone.
